# Polaris Documentation

> User and developer documentation for Polaris Finance — the self-scaling stablecoin operating system. Mint pAssets backed entirely by onchain collateral and yield. No T-bills. No CEXs. No compromises.

## Polaris Finance Documentation

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/

# Polaris Finance Documentation

Polaris is the **pETH-powered yield layer for all of DeFi**, a self-scaling stablecoin operating system. It produces yield-bearing **pETH** and uses it as the base collateral for minting synthetic assets (**pAssets**) through onchain debt positions.

The infrastructure is trustless and immutable, and every pAsset is backed entirely by onchain collateral and yield, with no T-bill or CEX dependency. The result is a competitive, risk-adjusted yield source for both ETH and stablecoins. Through **Flows**, third-party protocols can build on Polaris assets and share in protocol yield.

This documentation explains how the system works, how to use it, and the trade-offs it makes. For the current network, contract, and actionability status, start with [Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status).

## Launch timeline

Image: Polaris launch timeline. Early Research in 2024. Team Formation in June 2025. Testnet 1, private, March 2026. Testnet 2, public, May 2026, the current phase. Mainnet, forthcoming.

Image: Polaris system overview: ETH swaps into pETH on the bonding curve, pETH collateralizes CDPs that mint pUSD, and burning pETH for POLAR raises the floor and releases ETH.

## The three engines

Polaris is three products that each stand on their own, but whose real power is their synergy. The bonding curve makes pETH work. The CDP makes pAssets work. The conversion mechanism and POLAR make them work together:

Engine | What it is | Why it matters

**1. pETH** | ETH deposited into a two-way **bonding curve**, with a floor price designed to rise under the curve invariants | A capital-efficient, yield-bearing collateral with a contract-defined exit path under stated assumptions
**2. pAssets** | Synthetic pegged (USD, Gold, etc.) assets minted against collateral, via a Collateralized Debt Position (CDP) | Counterparty-free stables that scale with onchain activity
**3. POLAR** | The stewardship token, minted only by burning pETH in a **conversion auction** | Aligns long-term incentives and steers the protocol within hardcoded bounds

## Timed explainers

Timed explainers:
- [Overview](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/explainers/2-min): 2 min read.
- [Presentation](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/explainers/5-min): 5 min read.
- [Full Introduction](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/explainers/10-min): 10 min read.

## Popular task shortcuts

If you want to... | Go to

Use the public testnet app safely | [Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) and [Safety and Verification](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/safety-verification)
Get pETH or swap test WETH | [Swap](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/swap)
Borrow pUSD or pGOLD | [Borrow](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/borrow)
Deposit into the Stability Pool | [Earn](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/earn) or [Zap](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/zap)
Split pETH into fpETH and vpETH | [Split](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/split)
Use lower-level tools | [Advanced](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/advanced)
Build on Polaris | [Develop](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/develop)

## A note on this documentation

Polaris is a developing protocol still in testnet. This documentation reflects the current Public Testnet 1 state as accurately as possible.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: app, official app, testnet, Testnet 2, Sepolia, pETH, pAssets.

## Changelog

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/changelog

# Changelog

This is a changelog for **this documentation**, not the Polaris protocol. It records what changed in these pages — structure, content, and accessibility — so returning readers can see what is new.
Entries follow [Keep a Changelog](https://keepachangelog.com), use ISO dates (YYYY-MM-DD), and are listed newest first.

## [2026-06-08]

A documentation best-practices pass that upgrades existing pages without changing the protocol model.

### Added

- Point-of-decision risk notes on the action pages (open a trove, redemptions, Stability Pool deposits, conversions), each linking to the full Risk Disclosure.
- A POLAR tokenomics page covering distribution, allocations, and vesting.
- The Polaris system overview diagram on Core Concepts and the lineage diagram on Why Polaris, each with descriptive alt text, a caption, and a numbered text description so the meaning survives without the image.
- Developer **Gotchas** and **Failure modes** sections, plus a worked integration code example, in the developer pages.
- An `llms.txt` machine-readable export so integrators and agents can consume the docs programmatically.
- This documentation changelog.

### Changed

- Enriched the six role paths from thin link lists into role-specific reading sequences, each with a "Who this is for" line, prerequisites, and a one-line note on why each step matters.
- Added audience signposting that routes developers and users to the right starting point.
- Accessibility pass across the affected pages, including image alternatives and text descriptions for every embedded diagram.
- Trimmed over-long meta descriptions so search snippets don't truncate.
- Consolidated repeated cross-page explanations (conversion and floor mechanics, the stewardship "what it cannot do" list, Fee Router routing, the Recovery Mode interaction) so each concept is defined once and referenced elsewhere.

---

## Polaris Core Concepts

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/core-concepts

# Polaris Core Concepts

A single primitive, the bonding curve, produces pETH. pETH collateralizes every pAsset. Activity on any pAsset generates fees; fees burn pETH and raise its floor; a rising floor strengthens every position. POLAR captures the flywheel's long-term value and steers it.

This page is the map. Each term is defined in a sentence or two and links to its full treatment. If you read nothing else, read this.

## The assets

A synthetic debt asset minted from a Polaris CDP (pUSD, pGOLD, pCHF, …). Each is overcollateralized by pETH and counterparty-free. See [The pAsset Catalog](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/passet-catalog).

The flagship pAsset, pegged to the US dollar, backed by pETH collateral. See [Minting pAssets](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/minting-passets).

ETH deposited into a two-way [bonding curve](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/bonding-curve). It is not CDP-minted debt; it is the <em>only</em> collateral Polaris accepts, has a floor price that is designed to rise under the curve invariants, and is yield-bearing.

The protocol's stewardship token. After a fixed distribution at launch, new POLAR can be minted <em>only</em> by burning pETH in a [conversion auction](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions). <strong>vePOLAR</strong>, a vote-escrow layer for stewardship rights, is forthcoming.

## Minting & positions

Collateralized Debt Position: the mechanism behind minting. You post collateral and mint the pAsset directly from the protocol, not by borrowing another user's funds. See [Minting pAssets](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/minting-passets).

Your individual borrowing position in a Polaris CDP. Each trove is isolated, with its own collateral, debt, and collateralization ratio. See [Managing Your Trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/managing-your-trove).

Polaris is a <em>factory</em> of CDP instances, each issuing a different pAsset but all sharing the same pETH collateral and bonding curve.

## Keeping the peg

Exchanging a pAsset for its underlying collateral at face value (minus a fee) when it trades <em>below</em> peg. Restores the floor. See [Redemptions](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/redemptions).

The inverse of redemption. Arbitrageurs add pETH to troves to mint pAssets when the pAsset trades <em>above</em> peg. Restores the ceiling.

Redemption and minting volume serve as onchain proxy signals for the peg, letting Polaris set rates without an external oracle.

## Risk states

If a trove falls below its minimum collateralization ratio, it is liquidated, and the [Stability Pool](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield/stability-pool) absorbs the debt. See [Liquidations](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations).

A protocol-wide protective state entered when the system-wide reserve-backing-to-debt ratio (the [TCR](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/glossary)) falls below a fixed threshold. See [Recovery Mode](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode).

The mathematically enforced minimum price of pETH (in ETH), and that floor as a percentage of market price (your maximum drawdown). See [The Floor Price](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price).

## Yield & coordination

A reserve of pAssets that processes liquidations and is the primary venue for earning yield. See [The Stability Pool](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield/stability-pool).

The mechanism that mints POLAR by burning pETH; the most protocol-synergetic action possible. See [Conversion Auctions](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions).

Receives pETH from conversions and bonding-curve swap fees, then buffers and distributes it as yield to troves, pro-rata to their debt. See [The Fee Router](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/fee-router).

A stewardship role steers <em>quantitative parameters within the bounds the contracts define</em>. It does not govern the immutable core. See [Stewardship, not Governance](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/stewardship).

## The shape of the system

Image: Polaris system overview: ETH swaps into pETH on the bonding curve, pETH collateralizes CDPs that mint pUSD, and burning pETH for POLAR raises the floor and releases ETH

*Key takeaway: one collateral primitive feeds the whole system; pETH creates collateral, CDPs mint pAssets, and POLAR conversion raises the pETH floor.*

The diagram traces four steps and an outcome:

1. **Swap.** ETH enters the **bonding curve** and is issued as **pETH**; the curve captures volatility.
2. **Collateral.** pETH is deposited as the sole collateral for **CDPs**.
3. **Borrow.** A CDP mints **pUSD** (or another pAsset) against that pETH, for scalable onchain yield.
4. **Stewardship.** Burning pETH in a conversion auction mints **POLAR**, raising the pETH floor price and releasing ETH.

> Adoption ⇒ activity ⇒ velocity ⇒ fees ⇒ yield ⇒ more demand.

Next steps:
- [Getting Started](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/getting-started): The shortest path from "I have ETH" to "I am using Polaris."
- [Minting pAssets](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/minting-passets): How the CDP works from first principles.
- [Why Polaris?](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/why-polaris): The stablecoin trilemma and how Polaris resolves it.

## Getting Started with Polaris

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/getting-started

# Getting Started with Polaris

The shortest path from "I have ETH" to "I am using Polaris." Each step links to its full treatment.

Polaris is in public Public Testnet 1 on Sepolia (`11155111`). The official testnet app is [app.testnet.polarisfinance.io](https://app.testnet.polarisfinance.io/). Production Ethereum mainnet (`1`) is forthcoming, and no production app or mainnet contracts are published yet. Check [Polaris Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) and [Public Testnet 1](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet) before connecting a wallet or treating any page as mainnet-actionable.

## Choose your path

Building on Polaris? Jump to the [Develop](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/develop) path. Just using Polaris? Stay here and pick a journey below.

If you already know why you're here, start with the matching journey:

- **Borrow pAssets** — get pETH, open a trove, mint a pAsset, and manage liquidation risk: [Borrow pAssets](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/borrow-passets).
- **Earn yield** — deposit pAssets into a Stability Pool or understand Fee Router yield: [Earn Yield](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/earn-yield).
- **Hold or use pETH** — understand the bonding curve, floor price, and external collateral use: [Hold or Use pETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/hold-use-peth).
- **Convert and lock POLAR** — burn pETH to mint POLAR, lock as vePOLAR, and participate in bounded stewardship: [Convert and Lock POLAR](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/convert-lock-polar).
- **Develop** — plan an integration, indexer, vault, analytics surface, or risk tool: [Develop](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/develop).
- **Launch status and safety** — confirm official app, network, contracts, audits, and launch status before interacting: [Polaris Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) and [Safety and Verification](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/safety-verification).

## What you need

- **An Ethereum wallet** — any standard EOA or smart-account wallet.
- **For Public Testnet 1:** Sepolia ETH for gas, plus test WETH/reserve tokens from the app faucet when the faucet is enabled for your wallet. Testnet assets have no monetary value.
- **For production later:** ETH on Ethereum mainnet — the only asset you need to begin; Polaris converts it to [pETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/bonding-curve) for you.

That's the whole list. No KYC, no whitelist, no offchain account.

## Sepolia ETH, test WETH, and pETH

On Public Testnet 1, three "ETH-like" assets can appear in the flow:

- **Sepolia ETH** pays gas on Sepolia. Get it from a public Sepolia faucet; it is not mainnet ETH.
- **Test WETH / reserve token** is the mock reserve token in the Sepolia manifest. The app faucet can mint it when enabled for your wallet. It is not mainnet WETH and has no value.
- **pETH** is the Polaris bonding-curve collateral token. On Public Testnet 1, it is Sepolia-only pETH created from the test reserve token; it is not production pETH.

The first testnet path is: open the [Public Testnet 1 app](https://app.testnet.polarisfinance.io/), switch to Sepolia, mint test WETH/reserve tokens if the faucet is available, swap them through the bonding curve for pETH, borrow or mint a Testnet-active pAsset, then optionally deposit that pAsset into the Stability Pool to test Earn.

## The basic flow

In one line: **deposit ETH ⇒ receive pETH ⇒ open a trove and mint a pAsset ⇒ optionally deposit to the Stability Pool to earn yield.** On Public Testnet 1 this uses Sepolia test assets; in production it uses Ethereum mainnet once launch status says mainnet is live. Step by step:

1. **Deposit or swap into pETH.** On Public Testnet 1, use the app faucet for mock WETH/reserve tokens when available, then use the bonding-curve / swap surface to receive pETH. In production later, this starts from mainnet ETH.
2. **Receive pETH from the [bonding curve](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/bonding-curve).** Your input asset is swapped for pETH at the curve's current price: mock WETH/reserve tokens on Public Testnet 1, and ETH in production later. pETH is an ETH proxy with a floor price that only rises, and the only collateral Polaris accepts.
3. **Open a [trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/open-a-trove) and mint a pAsset.** Post your pETH as collateral, then choose which pAsset to mint. pUSD and pGOLD are Testnet-active; pCHF and pBigMac are illustrative or future catalog examples. The pAsset lands in your wallet; the debt stays in your trove. See [the catalog](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/passet-catalog) for what you can mint.
4. **Optionally, earn yield.** Deposit your pAsset into the [Stability Pool](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield/stability-pool) to earn a share of borrower interest plus discounted pETH from liquidations. This is optional — you can instead hold, LP, or use your pAsset anywhere in DeFi.

## Where the app lives

[app.testnet.polarisfinance.io](https://app.testnet.polarisfinance.io/) is the official public Public Testnet 1 app on Sepolia only. Production/mainnet app status remains **not live** until [Polaris Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) publishes otherwise.

## Launch verification

Before you connect a wallet or sign a transaction, verify:

- For Public Testnet 1, the app URL is exactly [https://app.testnet.polarisfinance.io/](https://app.testnet.polarisfinance.io/).
- For production/mainnet, wait until [Polaris Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) publishes the production app URL.
- The app is connected to the intended network: Sepolia (`11155111`) for Public Testnet 1, or Ethereum mainnet (`1`) only after production launch.
- Testnet contract addresses match [Public Testnet 1](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet) and the linked Sepolia manifests.
- Production contract addresses, verified source, ABIs, and audit status match [Contracts and Addresses](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/contracts) and [Audits and Security](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/audits-security) once production launches.
- The product page you're following has been updated from conceptual flow to launch parameters.

Next steps:
- [Core Concepts](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/core-concepts): The one-page map of the entire Polaris system.
- [Open a Trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/open-a-trove): Step-by-step guide to opening your first trove.
- [The Bonding Curve](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/bonding-curve): Understand how pETH is created and priced.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: app, official app, connect wallet, testnet, Sepolia, Sepolia ETH, WETH faucet, borrow, earn, swap.

## Polaris Launch Status

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status

# Polaris Launch Status

This page is the source of truth for whether Polaris docs are conceptual, testnet-actionable, or production-actionable.

Polaris is in <strong>public Public Testnet 1</strong>. Production mainnet is forthcoming.

## Current status

Item | Current status

Public phase | **Public Testnet 1**
Testnet network | **Sepolia**, chain ID `11155111`
Production network | **Ethereum mainnet**, chain ID `1`, forthcoming
Official Public Testnet 1 app | [https://app.testnet.polarisfinance.io/](https://app.testnet.polarisfinance.io/)
Production / mainnet app | Not live or published in these docs
Canonical production contracts | Not published yet
Testnet contracts and parameters | Published for Sepolia on [Public Testnet 1](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet); not canonical production addresses
Final audit reports | Not published in these docs yet
Bug bounty / disclosure program | Not public yet

Use app.testnet.polarisfinance.io only on Sepolia (<code>11155111</code>) with test assets. Testnet assets have no monetary value, and production/mainnet use is not live.

## What is actionable now

Action | Status | Use this rule

Read the protocol model | Conceptual now | The explainers, core concepts, and risk pages are safe to read as the intended model.
Test on Public Testnet 1 | Testnet-actionable now | Use Sepolia test assets and [Public Testnet 1](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet) manifests. Treat addresses, mocks, and parameters as replaceable.
Open testnet app | Testnet-actionable now | Use the official testnet interface at [app.testnet.polarisfinance.io](https://app.testnet.polarisfinance.io/).
Testnet Analytics | Testnet-actionable now | Detailed breakdown live from testnet data at [analytics.testnet.polarisfinance.io](https://analytics.testnet.polarisfinance.io/#/overview-v2).
Use production capital | Not yet mainnet-actionable | Wait until this page links the production app, production contracts, verified source, final parameters, and audit status.
Integrate contracts | Plan now, bind later | Build verification gates and schemas now. Do not hardcode unpublished production addresses, ABIs, events, or parameters.

## Network truth

Public Testnet 1 runs on **Sepolia (`11155111`)** and may use mock assets, mock oracles, redeployable contracts, and testnet-only parameters. It is for testing behavior, not for valuing production risk. The current app URL, active testnet pAssets, manifests, addresses, and Sepolia parameters live on [Public Testnet 1](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet).

Production Polaris is intended for **Ethereum mainnet (`1`)**, but mainnet app and contract addresses are not live or published yet. Any frontend, token, contract address, airdrop, support message, or bounty page claiming production status before it is linked here should be treated as unverified.

## Before any transaction

Do not connect a wallet or sign a transaction unless all relevant checks pass:

- For Public Testnet 1, the app URL is exactly [https://app.testnet.polarisfinance.io/](https://app.testnet.polarisfinance.io/).
- For production/mainnet, wait until this page publishes a production app URL.
- The wallet network matches the intended environment: Sepolia for Public Testnet 1, Ethereum mainnet only after production launch.
- The contract address appears in [Contracts & Addresses](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/contracts), [Public Testnet 1](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet), or the official deployment manifest you are using.
- Explorer source and ABI match the documented contract.
- The relevant product page has final values for the parameters your action depends on.
- Audit and disclosure status are clear on [Audits & Security](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/audits-security).

## Page labels used in these docs

- **Conceptual only** means the page explains the model and risks, but does not give enough live parameters to sign safely.
- **Testnet-actionable** means you can try the flow on Sepolia with test assets and testnet manifests.
- **Mainnet-actionable later** means the page needs production app links, verified addresses, final parameters, and audit status before use with real value.

For phishing and user-safety checks, continue to [Safety and Verification](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/safety-verification). For the intended deployment shape, see [Contracts & Addresses](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/contracts).

Relevant app/search vocabulary: app, official app, testnet app, app.testnet.polarisfinance.io, testnet, Testnet 2, Sepolia, chain ID 11155111, WETH faucet, contracts, audits.

---

## Why Polaris Finance

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/why-polaris

# Why Polaris Finance

## The trilemma

Stablecoins are said to be **scalable, yield-bearing, or trustless — pick two**. Market structure mostly reinforces that framing: the largest stablecoins rely on issuers, custodians, offchain assets, or other counterparties, while cleaner trustless designs stay smaller.

That trade-off is real, but it is not a law of nature. It exists because, for years, high offchain interest rates made it *easy* to back a stablecoin with T-bills and CEX yield and bring that yield onchain. Easy, but not trustless — and not innovative.

## The degradation pattern

The deeper problem is **structural drift toward centralization**. Stablecoins tend to start relatively clean and get dirtier in how they source yield as they grow, because centralized backing is the path of least resistance to scale.

Feature | **USDC** | **USDS / DAI** | **USDe** | **LUSD / BOLD** | **pUSD**

Issuer dependency | Centralized issuer with freeze/blacklist | Governance-set rates, RWA exposure | Market-neutral, custody-dependent | Counterparty-free | Counterparty-free
Yield source | Offchain reserves (not passed to holders) | Mixed: crypto + RWA + governance | Counterparty-dependent infrastructure | Limited onchain sources | Onchain protocol activity
Scalability constraint | Banking relationships | Governance decisions | Counterparty capacity | Capital inefficiency | Bonding-curve scaling
Immutable core | No | Partial | No | Yes | Yes

The lesson: a stablecoin built for trustlessness must be <em>architected</em> so degradation cannot happen, not merely intend to stay clean.

## The counterparty-free lineage

Polaris descends from the line of immutable, counterparty-free CDP stablecoins, built to fix the limitation each prior generation hit.

Image: Polaris lineage: SAI (2017) ⇒ LUSD (2021) ⇒ BOLD / Liquity V2 (2024) ⇒ pUSD (2026)

*The counterparty-free CDP lineage, 2017 to present.*

In text, the lineage reads:

1. **2017 — SAI:** the first CDP stablecoin, single-collateral. It later upgraded to DAI and then drifted toward centralized backing.
2. **2021 — LUSD:** immutable and decentralized, but capital-inefficient, and spent roughly 20% of its life more than 1% above peg.
3. **2024 — BOLD (Liquity V2):** a refined CDP mechanism from the original Liquity developers — still trustless, still yield-constrained.
4. **2026 — pUSD:** the next chapter. A bonding-curve collateral design that solves the overpeg problem and unlocks onchain-scaling yield without adding a counterparty.

## How Polaris resolves it

Polaris is built so the only counterparty you trust is **immutable code and Ethereum**:

- **Onchain-only yield.** Every yield source — borrower interest, bonding curve swap fees, conversion gains, PolarEX swap fees — is internal to the protocol and grows with activity. Nothing is rented from a CEX or T-bill desk.
- **Immutable core.** The bonding curve, CDP logic, liquidations, redemptions, and the Stability Pool are intended to be fixed in audited deployment code. No admin key can add a custodial PSM, external-collateral module, or freeze function under pressure.
- **Counterparty-free by construction.** pUSD sits in the same counterparty-free lineage as LUSD and BOLD — but adds yield that *scales* with DeFi volume rather than capping growth at what a counterparty can deliver.

Having a counterparty means capping your growth to whatever yield that counterparty can deliver.

Polaris turns onchain trading volume itself into a stablecoin yield source through its bonding curve, and extends the model to *any* asset through the [StablecoinOS](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/core-concepts). DeFi has had uncensorable-but-small and censorable-but-scalable stablecoins. Polaris is built to be the uncensorable, scalable one.

Next steps:
- [Core Concepts](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/core-concepts): A one-page map of every key term in the Polaris system.
- [Getting Started](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/getting-started): The shortest path from "I have ETH" to "I am using Polaris."
- [Minting pAssets](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/minting-passets): How the CDP works from first principles.

---

[Why Polaris?](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/why-polaris/)
Analyzing where USDC, USDS, USDe, and LUSD fall short — and how pUSD delivers scalable, yield-bearing stability without counterparty risk.

Time | Page | Use it when

## Polaris 10-Minute Introduction

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/explainers/10-min

10 min read · Full Introduction

# Polaris 10-Minute Introduction

## Where stablecoins break down

DeFi has had two kinds of stablecoin: centralized-but-scalable and trustless-but-small.

The pattern is structural. A project starts with relatively clean collateral (crypto assets, onchain yield); as it grows, it adds real-world assets, centralized stablecoins, or CEX-dependent yield to keep pace with demand. The economics push that way: T-bills and lending desks are large, reliable, and easy to integrate. Onchain alternatives are harder to scale.

The result: the largest stablecoins in DeFi are managed by entities that can freeze balances, blacklist addresses, and change collateral composition at will. They are useful, but not counterparty-free.

Meanwhile, the trustless designs — LUSD, BOLD — have proven immutable CDPs work. But without a scalable onchain yield source, their growth is constrained, their rates volatile, and they have stayed in a trustless-focused niche.

The open question is whether a stablecoin can be *both* trustless and scalable — one whose yield source grows with adoption, not one borrowed from a centralized counterparty. Polaris is built to answer it.

## The lineage

Polaris is not starting from scratch. It descends from a line of immutable, counterparty-free CDP stablecoins:

Image: Polaris counterparty-free CDP lineage: SAI (2017), LUSD (2021), BOLD / Liquity V2 (2024), pUSD (2026)

*Key takeaway: Polaris inherits the counterparty-free CDP lineage while changing the yield source from scarce redemption revenue to protocol activity.*

- **SAI (2017):** The first single-collateral CDP stablecoin. Proved the model works. Later became multi-collateral DAI, then drifted toward centralized backing — Real World Assets, USDC in the PSM, governance-controlled rates.
- **LUSD (2021):** Immutable, counterparty-free, governance-free. Years with no admin breach. But capital-inefficient: it spent roughly 20% of its life more than 1% above peg, and its yield was limited to redemption fee revenue.
- **BOLD (2024):** Liquity V2 — refined CDP mechanics, multi-collateral, still trustless. A genuine improvement, still yield-constrained.
- **pUSD (2026):** The next chapter. A bonding-curve collateral that solves the overpeg problem and produces yield scaling with DeFi volume, without adding a counterparty.

The lineage's lesson: immutability and counterparty-freedom are achievable and proven — SAI, LUSD, and BOLD showed that. The missing piece has always been a scalable onchain yield source.

## The bonding curve: the engine of everything

The bonding curve is what makes Polaris different from every prior CDP stablecoin.

When you deposit ETH into Polaris, the curve issues you **pETH** at the current price. When you redeem pETH, you get ETH back — at the floor price (at minimum), or the market price (if higher).

### The floor price

The floor price is the minimum exchange rate between pETH and ETH under the bonding-curve invariants. It is designed to rise as swap or conversion fees accumulate and a portion adjusts the floor upward. No normal protocol action lowers it.

The implication: pETH is an ETH proxy with a contract-defined lower bound in ETH terms, not a fixed-price ETH substitute. Its market price can move against ETH, but the floor-to-market ratio frames the curve-implied drawdown before gas, slippage, MEV, smart-contract risk, and chain-liveness risk.

If pETH's market price is 0.95 ETH and its floor is 0.85 ETH, your worst-case redemption is 0.85 ETH. As fees accumulate, that floor may rise to 0.87, then 0.90. It never falls.

### Onchain-only yield

The curve earns fees on every ETH↔pETH swap. Those fees raise the floor. So pETH's yield comes from trading volume — from real onchain activity — not from a T-bill desk or a CEX lending book.

The key insight: **the more DeFi activity flows through Polaris, the higher the floor rises, and the more valuable pETH becomes.** Yield scales with adoption instead of being capped by whatever a counterparty can deliver.

## pAssets: a factory of synthetic assets

With pETH as the collateral primitive, Polaris runs a factory of CDP (Collateralized Debt Position) instances. Each issues a different synthetic asset against the same collateral:

- **pUSD** — pegged to the US dollar
- **pGOLD** — pegged to gold
- **pCHF** — pegged to the Swiss franc
- (additional pAssets are deployable as new CDP instances)

### How a CDP works

You lock pETH in a **trove** and mint a pAsset against it. Your collateral ratio must stay above the minimum threshold for your trove type. If it falls below, your trove is liquidated — the Stability Pool absorbs the debt and takes your collateral at a discount.

The peg is maintained by two opposing arbitrage forces:

**Below peg:** Anyone can redeem pAssets for pETH at face value (minus a small fee). If the system is under-backed, redemptions are scaled down in proportion to the reserve backing. This burns pAssets, reduces supply, and drives the price back up.

**Above peg:** Anyone can open a trove, mint pAssets cheaply, and sell them above peg for a profit. This increases supply and drives the price back down.

### Autonomous interest rates

Polaris sets borrower interest rates without an external price oracle. It uses redemption and minting volume as proxy signals:

- High redemption volume → the pAsset is trading below peg → rates rise to deter new minting.
- Low redemption volume → the peg is healthy → rates fall to encourage minting.

This makes rate-setting self-contained and tamper-proof.

### The Stability Pool

The Stability Pool is the safety net for every CDP. Holders deposit pAssets; when a trove is liquidated, the pool absorbs the debt and takes the collateral at a discount. Depositors earn:

1. A share of borrower interest from all CDPs.
2. Discounted pETH from liquidations.
3. Any additional protocol incentives, if enabled, are expected to come from routed hard-asset revenue rather than POLAR emissions.

It is the primary yield venue for pAsset holders and the mechanism that keeps the CDP system solvent.

## POLAR: stewardship through alignment

POLAR is the protocol's stewardship token. Apart from a one-time genesis allocation of 100,000,000 POLAR minted to the stewardship address at deployment, its issuance is deliberately constrained: **beyond that fixed initial supply, POLAR can only be minted by burning pETH in a conversion auction.** There is no other ongoing issuance path — no investor unlock, no inflationary mint function.

### What burning pETH in a conversion auction does

When pETH is burned:
1. The ETH backing the burned pETH is freed from the bonding curve's floor reserve — a configurable rebate goes to the converter and the remainder is recycled into the system (market-buying pETH to refill the reward buffer).
2. The pETH floor price rises (supply contraction).
3. POLAR is minted to the burner.

All three effects are positive for the system. Burning pETH is the highest-alignment action a user can take.

### vePOLAR and stewardship

A designated stewardship role can steer quantitative parameters — fee and interest-rate settings, the conversion parameters — within the bounds the immutable contracts define. It cannot modify the bonding curve logic, CDP mechanics, liquidation rules, or the fixed collateralization thresholds.

The distinction matters: Polaris has stewardship, not governance. The core is fixed; only the dials within it are adjustable.

## The system end to end

Image: Polaris system: ETH enters the bonding curve as pETH, pETH collateralizes CDPs that mint pAssets, burning pETH for POLAR raises the floor and releases ETH

*Key takeaway: ETH becomes pETH once, then pETH powers collateral, yield routing, and POLAR conversion across the system.*

The four-step picture:
1. **Swap.** ETH enters the bonding curve and is issued as pETH at the current price.
2. **Collateralize.** pETH is the only collateral every CDP instance accepts.
3. **Borrow.** A CDP mints a pAsset — pUSD, pGOLD, or any other — against that pETH.
4. **Stewardship.** Burning pETH for POLAR raises the floor, releases ETH, and aligns token holders with the protocol's long-term health.

## The flywheel

Image: Polaris flywheel: more adoption drives activity, activity generates fees, fees fund conversions, conversions raise the pETH floor, a higher floor strengthens collateral, stronger collateral enables more minting, which drives more adoption

*Key takeaway: the loop is endogenous; more useful pAssets create more pETH demand, more fees, and a stronger pETH floor under the stated assumptions.*

> Adoption → activity → fees → conversions → higher pETH floor → stronger collateral → more minting → more adoption.

Each component reinforces the others:

- More pAsset CDPs create demand for pETH.
- Demand for pETH generates bonding curve swap fees.
- Fees raise the pETH floor.
- A higher floor makes every existing CDP position safer (the collateral is worth more in ETH terms).
- Safer positions lower the protocol's risk profile, attracting more users.
- More users generate more activity, and the loop continues.

This compounding is structural, not dependent on token emissions.

## Three intended invariants

Invariant | How it is enforced

**pETH floor is designed to rise** | Bonding curve math — no normal protocol action in the intended implementation lowers it
**No counterparty in the collateral stack** | pETH is backed by ETH; pAssets are backed by pETH; nothing else enters
**Immutable core** | Bonding curve, CDP logic, liquidations, and redemptions are fixed in bytecode; there is no admin key and no upgrade function for the core

These are not marketing promises. They are intended invariants that must be verified against the audited production contracts once addresses and reports are published.

## Risk model

Understanding Polaris means knowing what can and cannot happen:

**What can happen:**
- Your trove can be liquidated if your collateral ratio falls below the minimum. You keep any surplus above the liquidation threshold — liquidations are not a total loss.
- Recovery Mode triggers when the protocol's reserve backing relative to total debt (its reserve-to-debt ratio) falls below a threshold. While active, borrowing tightens — actions that increase debt or reduce collateral must meet a higher minimum collateral ratio and may not worsen a position's ratio — but liquidation eligibility stays at the normal minimum collateral ratio, and pure collateral top-ups and repayments remain allowed.
- pETH's market price can fall against ETH and with ETH. The floor defines a curve-level lower-bound quote under stated assumptions; it does not protect against ETH falling against other units.

**What cannot happen by design, once the intended production contracts are verified:**
- No normal protocol action can lower the pETH floor under the intended curve invariant.
- The CDP or bonding curve logic cannot be upgraded.
- Your pAsset balance cannot be frozen or confiscated.

---

Where to go next depends on what you want to do.

- Ready to use it? [Getting Started](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/getting-started)
- Want the concept glossary? [Core Concepts](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/core-concepts)
- Understand the bonding curve in depth: [The Bonding Curve](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/bonding-curve) and [The Floor Price](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price)
- Mint your first pUSD: [Open a Trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/open-a-trove)
- Earn yield: [The Stability Pool](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield/stability-pool)
- Build on Polaris: [Develop](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/develop)

---

## Polaris 2-Minute Overview

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/explainers/2-min

2 min read · Overview

# Polaris 2-Minute Overview

Polaris is a **self-scaling stablecoin operating system** — trustless, immutable infrastructure for minting synthetic assets backed entirely by onchain collateral and yield. No T-bills. No CEXs. No counterparties.

## One primitive, three products

Everything flows from a single primitive: the **bonding curve**.

**pETH** is ETH deposited into a two-way bonding curve. It is the only collateral Polaris accepts. Its floor price — the contract-defined minimum redemption price under the curve assumptions — is designed to rise. pETH is yield-bearing: swap fees accrue to the protocol and raise that floor over time.

**pAssets** (pUSD, pGOLD, …) are synthetic assets minted against pETH via a CDP (Collateralized Debt Position). You lock pETH; the protocol mints a pAsset into your wallet. No counterparty holds your position. The logic is immutable.

**POLAR** is the stewardship token. Beyond a fixed supply minted once at launch, the only way to create more is to burn pETH in a conversion auction. Through stewardship, POLAR holders steer protocol parameters within the bounds the immutable contracts define — they do not control the immutable core.

## The flywheel

The three products compound:

Image: Polaris flywheel: adoption drives activity, activity generates fees, fees enable conversions, conversions raise the pETH floor, a higher floor strengthens collateral, stronger collateral enables more minting

*Key takeaway: activity funds the system directly, so growth reinforces collateral quality instead of depending on token emissions.*

> More pAsset activity → fees → conversions → higher pETH floor → stronger collateral → more minting.

Growth reinforces the system rather than diluting it.

## Why it is different

- **Immutable core.** Bonding curve, CDP logic, liquidations, and redemptions are fixed in code. No admin key.
- **Onchain-only yield.** Every yield source (borrower interest, swap fees, conversion gains) is internal to the protocol and grows with DeFi activity.
- **Contract-defined floor.** pETH's floor is designed to only rise under the bonding-curve invariants. Your curve-implied downside in ETH terms shrinks as the floor catches up.

That is the whole system. Three products, one flywheel, one immutable core.

---

**Go deeper →** [5-Minute Presentation](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/explainers/5-min) · [Core Concepts](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/core-concepts) · [Getting Started](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/getting-started)

---

## Polaris 5-Minute Presentation

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/explainers/5-min

5 min read · Presentation

# Polaris 5-Minute Presentation

## The problem

Stablecoins have always faced a structural trilemma: **scalable, yield-bearing, or trustless — pick two.**

The largest stablecoins are scalable and yield-bearing but rely on centralized issuers with freeze and blacklist powers. Trustless designs are counterparty-free but capital-inefficient and yield-constrained. The pattern is consistent: as a stablecoin scales, it drifts toward centralized yield, the path of least resistance.

Polaris is designed so drift cannot happen. Its yield sources are *internal* to the protocol, growing with onchain activity. Its core logic is immutable, with no admin key able to add a counterparty under pressure.

## The counterparty-free lineage

Polaris descends from the line of immutable CDP stablecoins:

Image: Polaris lineage: SAI (2017) → LUSD (2021) → BOLD / Liquity V2 (2024) → pUSD (2026)

*Key takeaway: Polaris keeps the immutable CDP lineage and adds an onchain yield source that scales with usage.*

Each generation fixed a flaw the previous hit. SAI proved CDPs could work; LUSD proved they could be immutable; BOLD refined the mechanism. Polaris extends the lineage by solving the *yield-scaling* constraint: the bonding curve converts trading volume directly into protocol revenue, so yield grows with adoption rather than being rented from a CEX.

## The three engines

### pETH — collateral with a rising floor

pETH is ETH deposited into a two-way bonding curve. Deposit ETH and you receive pETH at the current price; redeem and you get back ETH through the curve, with the floor as the lower-bound quote under the curve assumptions.

The bonding curve's math is designed to enforce two things:

1. **The floor price rises under normal protocol operation.** As swap fees accumulate, the floor adjusts upward. No normal protocol action lowers it.
2. **pETH is yield-bearing.** Every fee the curve earns narrows the gap between pETH's floor and its market price — real yield from real activity, no counterparty required.

pETH is the *only* collateral Polaris accepts. This is a feature: every pAsset is ultimately backed by ETH, with nothing else in the collateral stack.

### pAssets — synthetic assets from CDPs

pAssets (pUSD, pGOLD, pCHF, …) are synthetic assets minted against pETH collateral via CDPs. You lock pETH in a trove; the protocol mints a pAsset into your wallet.

A pAsset trades near its peg because two forces bound it:

- **Below peg:** Anyone can redeem pAssets for pETH at face value, arbitraging the price back up.
- **Above peg:** Anyone can open a trove and mint more pAssets cheaply, arbitraging the price back down.

Interest rates adjust autonomously: redemption and minting volume serve as onchain proxy signals, no external oracle needed. Polaris is a *factory* of CDPs: the same logic can issue pUSD, pGOLD, or any synthetic asset.

### POLAR — stewardship, not speculation

POLAR is the protocol's stewardship token. Aside from a fixed initial supply minted once to stewardship at deployment, the only way to create more is to burn pETH in a conversion auction — no investor unlock, no inflationary mint.

Burning pETH in a conversion auction does three things at once:
1. Frees the ETH backing those pETH from the bonding-curve reserve — partly rebated to the converter, partly recycled into protocol rewards.
2. Raises the pETH floor (supply reduction).
3. Mints POLAR to the converter.

POLAR confers stewardship: a designated stewardship role can steer a small set of quantitative parameters (fee, interest, and conversion settings), but cannot touch the immutable core — the bonding curve, CDP logic, liquidations, and redemptions. Stewardship, not governance.

## The system end to end

Image: Polaris system: ETH enters the bonding curve as pETH, pETH collateralizes CDPs that mint pAssets, burning pETH for POLAR raises the floor and releases ETH

*Key takeaway: pETH is the shared collateral layer; every pAsset instance and POLAR conversion loops back into that same base.*

Four steps:
1. **Swap.** ETH enters the bonding curve and is issued as pETH.
2. **Collateralize.** pETH is deposited as the sole collateral in a CDP.
3. **Borrow.** The CDP mints a pAsset (pUSD, pGOLD, or any other).
4. **Stewardship.** Burning pETH for POLAR raises the floor and releases ETH.

## The flywheel

Image: Polaris flywheel: adoption drives activity, fees enable conversions, conversions raise the floor, a higher floor strengthens collateral, stronger collateral enables more minting

*Key takeaway: fees and conversions strengthen pETH, and stronger pETH makes future borrowing and minting safer under the curve assumptions.*

> Adoption → activity → fees → conversions → higher pETH floor → stronger collateral → more minting.

Each component reinforces the others. More pAsset CDPs create demand for pETH; demand generates curve fees; fees raise the floor; a higher floor makes every position safer; safer positions attract more users.

## Three intended invariants

Invariant | Enforcement

pETH floor is designed to rise | Bonding curve math — no normal protocol action lowers it
No counterparty in the collateral stack | pETH is backed by ETH; nothing else enters
Immutable core | Bonding curve, CDPs, liquidations, and redemptions are intended to be fixed in audited deployment code

---

**Go deeper →** [10-Minute Introduction](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/explainers/10-min) for full mechanics · [Getting Started](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/getting-started) to use the protocol

---

## Polaris User Paths

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths

# Polaris User Paths

Start with the path that matches the action you want to take, but check [Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) first. Polaris is currently public Public Testnet 1 on Sepolia; production mainnet actions are not yet live.

## Start here if

If you want to... | Read

Verify the official app, network, addresses, and audit status | [Polaris Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status), then [Safety and Verification](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/safety-verification)
Mint pUSD, pGOLD, or another pAsset | [Borrow pAssets](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/borrow-passets)
Earn pAsset or pETH-denominated yield | [Earn Yield](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/earn-yield)
Hold pETH or understand the bonding curve first | [Hold or Use pETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/hold-use-peth)
Convert pETH, stake POLAR, or study future vePOLAR locking | [Convert / Stake POLAR](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/convert-lock-polar)
Build an app, indexer, vault, or risk system | [Develop on Polaris](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/develop)

## Actionability

These paths are decision guides, not production launch instructions until [Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) lists the official app, production contracts, final parameters, and audit status. For now, builders can plan integrations and test on Sepolia with testnet manifests.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: Dashboard, Swap, Borrow, Earn, Split, Zap, Guide, Advanced, Analytics.

---

## Borrow Polaris pAssets

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/borrow-passets

# Borrow Polaris pAssets

**Who this is for:** anyone who wants to mint pUSD, pGOLD, or another pAsset against pETH collateral and actively manage that position.

**Prerequisites:** an Ethereum wallet, test assets on Sepolia for Public Testnet 1 or ETH on Ethereum mainnet after production launch, and a working grasp of overcollateralization and liquidation. If those terms are new, read [Core Concepts](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/core-concepts) first.

> **Launch status:** Borrowing is testnet-actionable on Sepolia only when using the official Public Testnet 1 manifests. Production borrowing is not mainnet-actionable until [Polaris Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) publishes the app URL, production addresses, final collateral thresholds, and audit status. Do not connect to a frontend or contract that claims to be Polaris unless it is linked from these docs or another official Polaris channel.

> **Terminology:** In the app, **Borrow** is the task label. Docs call a pAsset debt position a **trove**; contracts may call the mechanism a **CDP**. **Reserve loans** are separate WETH loans against [fpETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/split), not pAsset troves.

## Reading sequence

Read these in order — each step builds context the next one assumes.

1. [Getting Started](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/getting-started) — confirms your wallet, network, and launch status so you avoid an unverified deployment.
2. [The Bonding Curve](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/bonding-curve) — pETH is the *only* collateral Polaris accepts; you must hold it before borrowing.
3. [Minting pAssets](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/minting-passets) — explains the CDP model and the peg mechanics that determine your interest rate.
4. [Open a Trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/open-a-trove) — the conceptual flow for posting collateral and minting your pAsset.
5. [Managing Your Trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/managing-your-trove) — how collateralization, debt, and interest move over time, and how to stay clear of liquidation.
6. [Liquidations](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations) and [Recovery Mode](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode) — the failure states that decide how conservatively to size your position.

## What to verify at launch

- The official app URL.
- The intended network: Sepolia (`11155111`) for Public Testnet 1, or Ethereum mainnet (`1`) after production launch.
- The pETH, pAsset, trove manager, and Stability Pool addresses.
- Final minimum collateralization, liquidation, redemption, and fee parameters for the pAsset you mint.
- Audit reports and verified contract source.

## Next

- Want yield on the pAsset after minting? Continue to [Earn Yield](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/earn-yield).
- Want to understand the collateral first? Continue to [Hold or Use pETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/hold-use-peth).
- Want the safety checklist? Continue to [Polaris Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) and [Safety and Verification](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/safety-verification).

Relevant app/search vocabulary: Borrow, borrow, mint, open trove, pUSD, pGOLD, ICR, official app.

---

## Convert, Stake, and Future-Lock POLAR

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/convert-lock-polar

# Convert, Stake, and Future-Lock POLAR

**Who this is for:** long-term participants who want to acquire POLAR through conversion, use current POLAR Staking where available, and understand forthcoming vePOLAR locking.

**Prerequisites:** pETH to burn in a conversion auction, and acceptance that conversion is one-way — you cannot un-burn pETH. To understand what your burn contributes, read [The Floor Price](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price) first.

> **Launch status:** Conversion and current staking are testnet-actionable only where Sepolia artifacts exist. Production conversion, staking, and vePOLAR locking are not mainnet-actionable until [Polaris Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) publishes the official app, POLAR, staking, and vePOLAR addresses, lock durations, voting process, final parameter bounds, and audit status. Do not use an unofficial conversion, staking, or locking interface.

## Reading sequence

Read these in order — understand the token and the irreversible burn before committing pETH.

1. [The POLAR Token](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/polar-token) — POLAR's role and why, after the initial distribution, new POLAR can be minted *only* by burning pETH.
2. [Conversion Auctions](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions) — the mechanism and its risks; read before burning any pETH to mint POLAR.
3. [Participate in a Conversion](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/participate-in-conversion) — the conceptual user flow for a conversion.
4. [vePOLAR](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/vepolar) — how future locking will differ from current POLAR Staking.
5. [Stewardship, not Governance](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/stewardship) — the hardcoded bounds your vote operates within; read before voting on any proposal.

## POLAR Staking vs vePOLAR

POLAR Staking is the current app staking/rewards surface where exposed. vePOLAR is forthcoming vote-escrow locking. Once live, vePOLAR can steward bounded parameters, vote on permitted onboarding decisions, and receive a stewarded share of protocol yield. It cannot upgrade contracts, change the bonding curve, change liquidation or redemption logic, create emissions gauges, or mint POLAR except through pETH conversion.

## What to verify at launch

- The official POLAR, conversion auction, and vePOLAR addresses.
- The official POLAR Staking address and claim path, if staking is exposed.
- Verified source and ABI for each contract.
- Final lock duration choices and vote-power decay rules.
- Final proposal, voting, quorum, timelock, and execution rules.
- The hardcoded parameter bounds for any proposal you vote on.

---

## Develop on Polaris

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/develop

# Develop on Polaris

**Who this is for:** engineers building an app, indexer, risk system, vault, liquidation monitor, analytics surface, or external market around Polaris.

**Prerequisites:** familiarity with onchain reads/writes (viem, ethers, or equivalent) and the Polaris product model. New to these concepts? Read [Core Concepts](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/core-concepts) first.

> **Launch status:** Polaris is in public Public Testnet 1 on Sepolia, with production mainnet forthcoming. Contract addresses, ABIs, verified source, canonical events, SDKs, subgraphs, and API endpoints for production are not yet published. Build against the documented model, but do not hardcode pre-launch addresses, method names, or event signatures. Track [Polaris Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) before binding to artifacts.

## Reading sequence

Read these in order — model the system before you bind to any artifact.

1. [Core Concepts](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/core-concepts) — the system model and its component boundaries.
2. [Contracts and Addresses](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/contracts) — the deployment shape (a shared core plus one instance per pAsset), the contract set, and the address, source, and ABI format to verify against.
3. [Audits and Security](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/audits-security) — the security model and audit status; read before treating a deployment as production-grade.
4. [Polaris Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) and [Safety and Verification](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/safety-verification) — the pre-interaction checklist for network, chain ID, addresses, and verified source.

## Build now, bind later

Until deployment artifacts ship, the safest useful work is interface planning:

- Model the product concepts: pETH, pAssets, troves, Stability Pools, Fee Router, POLAR, and vePOLAR.
- Design data schemas around stable conceptual entities, not unpublished function names.
- Build verification gates so your app refuses unverified networks or addresses.
- Keep chain-specific config external and replaceable.
- Track the official docs for the deployment manifest, ABIs, and event catalog.

## Gotchas

- **Testnet addresses are not production addresses.** Treat Sepolia manifests as replaceable fixtures. Never use them as defaults for mainnet.
- **One pAsset means one instance.** A pUSD trove and a pGOLD trove are unrelated positions with different managers, pools, parameters, and risk states.
- **pETH is collateral, not pAsset debt.** Do not put pETH in the same accounting bucket as CDP-minted pAssets.
- **Redemptions are global to an instance.** There is no sorted lowest-ICR queue. If you model borrower exposure, model pro-rata reduction across all open troves for that pAsset.
- **Fee Router pETH rewards and primary-interest splits are separate streams.** Do not assume every yield destination is paid by the same contract or denomination.
- **Final event names are pending.** Build your indexer so event bindings are generated from the published ABI, not copied from draft examples.

## Failure modes to handle

Failure mode | Integration response

Wrong chain ID | Refuse reads/writes and show the expected chain from [Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status).
Address missing from the manifest | Disable the feature rather than falling back to a search result or environment default.
Explorer source or ABI mismatch | Treat the deployment as unverified and fail closed.
Stale oracle or unavailable price path | Hide leverage/borrowing actions and show risk state as unknown.
Recovery Mode active | Block or warn on collateral-reducing and debt-increasing flows according to live contract state.
Redemption, liquidation, or conversion quote changes before inclusion | Requote before signing and use explicit user slippage bounds.
Unknown pAsset instance | Do not share pUSD assumptions with another pAsset; load per-instance parameters.

## Worked integration sketch

The exact package, ABI, and method names are pending. The important pattern is a fail-closed deployment gate before any read or write.

This is integration pseudo-code, not a published SDK. The shape is copy-safe TypeScript, but the explorer-verification implementation must come from your own stack or a future Polaris SDK after canonical ABIs publish.

```ts
type Address = `0x${string}`

type PolarisDeployment = {
  chainId: number
  contracts: Record<string, Address>
  abiHashes: Record<string, string>
}

type ReadClient = {
  getBytecode(args: { address: Address }): Promise<`0x${string}` | null | undefined>
}

type ExplorerVerifier = (args: {
  address: Address
  expectedAbiHash: string
}) => Promise<boolean>

const EXPECTED_CHAIN_ID = 11155111 // Public Testnet 1 today; production mainnet is 1 after launch.
```

```ts

  client: ReadClient,
  deployment: PolarisDeployment,
  verifyExplorerSourceAndAbiHash: ExplorerVerifier
) {
  if (deployment.chainId !== EXPECTED_CHAIN_ID) {
    throw new Error(`Unexpected Polaris network: ${deployment.chainId}`)
  }

  for (const [name, address] of Object.entries(deployment.contracts)) {
    const code = await client.getBytecode({ address })
    if (!code) throw new Error(`Missing bytecode for ${name}`)

    const verified = await verifyExplorerSourceAndAbiHash({
      address,
      expectedAbiHash: deployment.abiHashes[name]
    })
    if (!verified) throw new Error(`Unverified Polaris contract: ${name}`)
  }

  return {
    peth: deployment.contracts.peth,
    pUSDManager: deployment.contracts.pUSDManager,
    pUSDStabilityPool: deployment.contracts.pUSDStabilityPool
  }
}
```

Use the same shape for production later, but with Ethereum mainnet chain ID `1` and only after [Contracts & Addresses](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/contracts) publishes canonical production artifacts.

## Next

- Need the system model? See [Core Concepts](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/core-concepts).
- Need artifact expectations (addresses, source, ABIs)? See [Contracts and Addresses](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/contracts).
- Need safety gates before connecting to a deployment? See [Polaris Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) and [Safety and Verification](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/safety-verification).

---

## Earn Yield on Polaris

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/earn-yield

# Earn Yield on Polaris

**Who this is for:** holders who want protocol-native yield from pAssets, pETH, current POLAR Staking, or future locked POLAR — without token emissions, bribes, or offchain backing.

**Prerequisites:** a pAsset to deposit (so most readers come here after [Borrow pAssets](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/borrow-passets)) or pETH, plus awareness that Stability Pool yield carries liquidation-conversion exposure to your principal.

> **Launch status:** Yield flows are conceptual and testnet-actionable only where Sepolia artifacts exist. Production deposits are not mainnet-actionable until [Polaris Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) publishes the official app, production pool addresses, final splits, buffering parameters, and audit status.

## Reading sequence

Read these in order — start with where the yield originates before committing capital.

1. [Where the Yield Comes From](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield/yield-sources) — the full source map, so you can judge whether each yield stream is sustainable rather than subsidized.
2. [The Stability Pool](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield/stability-pool) — the primary venue for pAsset yield, and how liquidations can convert your deposit into discounted pETH.
3. [Deposit to the Stability Pool](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield/deposit-to-stability-pool) — the user flow you will follow once the app is public.
4. [The Fee Router](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/fee-router) — how pETH from fees and conversions reaches configured receivers, and how that differs from pAsset-denominated staking rewards.
5. [Flows](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/flows) — only if you are an integrator or operator seeking protocol revenue sharing; safe to skip as a plain depositor.

## What yield is not

Polaris yield is not a token-emission program, gauge market, bribe market, T-bill wrapper, or CEX lending strategy. It comes from protocol activity: borrower interest, liquidation gains, bonding-curve fees, conversion gains, and other onchain fees described in the yield docs.

## What to verify at launch

- The official app URL.
- The Stability Pool address for the pAsset you deposit.
- The final interest split and liquidation parameters for that pAsset.
- The Fee Router address and verified source.
- Whether any wrapper vault you use is official, audited, and listed by the project that operates it.

## Next

- Borrow first? Go to [Borrow pAssets](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/borrow-passets).
- Stake POLAR or study future vePOLAR locking? Go to [Convert / Stake POLAR](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/convert-lock-polar).
- Verify contracts before depositing? Go to [Polaris Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) and [Safety and Verification](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/safety-verification).

Relevant app/search vocabulary: Earn, earn, yield, APR, Stability Pool, deposit, claim rewards.

---

## Hold or Use Polaris pETH

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/hold-use-peth

# Hold or Use Polaris pETH

**Who this is for:** anyone who wants to hold pETH for its rising floor, use it as collateral, or understand the asset before borrowing against it.

**Prerequisites:** a wallet and the right ETH for the environment: Sepolia ETH for Public Testnet 1, or ETH on Ethereum mainnet after production launch. No trove or pAsset is required to hold pETH; borrowing is optional and comes later.

> **Launch status:** pETH is testnet-actionable only through the current Sepolia deployment manifests. Production pETH use is not mainnet-actionable until [Polaris Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) publishes the official app, production bonding-curve address, verified source, final curve parameters, and audit status. Do not trust unofficial addresses.

## Reading sequence

Read these in order to understand pETH and its downside bound before relying on it.

1. [The Bonding Curve](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/bonding-curve) — how ETH deposits become pETH, and where your exit liquidity comes from.
2. [The Floor Price](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price) — the contract-defined downside bound in ETH terms under the curve assumptions.
3. [Minting pAssets](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/minting-passets) — only if you may use pETH inside a trove; explains what changes when pETH becomes collateral.
4. [Where the Yield Comes From](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield/yield-sources) — how bonding-curve and conversion activity raises the floor for pETH holders.
5. [Risk Disclosure](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/risk-disclosure) — read before using pETH as collateral anywhere, inside or outside Polaris.

## pETH roles

Role | What to read

Hold pETH directly | [Bonding Curve](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/bonding-curve), [Floor Price](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price)
Borrow against pETH | [Borrow pAssets](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/borrow-passets)
Integrate pETH externally | [Develop](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/develop), [Core Concepts](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/core-concepts)
Verify the asset | [Polaris Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status), [Safety and Verification](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/safety-verification)

## What to verify at launch

- The official pETH contract address and verified source.
- The bonding-curve address on the intended network: Sepolia for Public Testnet 1, Ethereum mainnet after production launch.
- Final curve, fee, and floor-ratio parameters once published.
- The exit path from pETH back to ETH.
- Any external venue's risk controls before using pETH outside Polaris.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: Swap, swap, Split, fpETH, vpETH, pETH, floor price.

## Polaris Safety and Verification

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/paths/safety-verification

# Polaris Safety and Verification

**Who this is for:** every Polaris user and integrator; run this path before connecting a wallet, sending ETH, minting a pAsset, depositing into a pool, locking POLAR, or integrating contracts.

**Prerequisites:** none. Complete this path *first*, before any other journey, because impersonation and phishing are most likely around launch and testnet phases.

> **Launch status:** [Polaris Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) is the source of truth. As of June 8, 2026, public Public Testnet 1 is on Sepolia (`11155111`) and the official testnet app is [app.testnet.polarisfinance.io](https://app.testnet.polarisfinance.io/). Production Ethereum mainnet (`1`) is forthcoming, and no production app, production addresses, ABIs, verified source, final parameters, or audit reports are published yet.

> **Open Testnet App:** Use [https://app.testnet.polarisfinance.io/](https://app.testnet.polarisfinance.io/) only on Sepolia (`11155111`) with no-value test assets. Treat any production frontend, token, contract, airdrop, or support message as unverified until it is linked from [Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status).

App anchors: **Dashboard** first for network and system state, then any action tab's pre-confirmation screen for contract, token, amount, fee, approval, and simulation checks.

## Verification checklist

Work top to bottom; each row names a check and where it will be confirmed at launch.

Check | Where it will be confirmed

Current launch phase and network | [Polaris Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status)
Testnet app URL and production app status | [Polaris Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) and [Getting Started](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/getting-started)
Deployed addresses | [Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status), official Sepolia manifests, and [Contracts and Addresses](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/contracts) once production is live
Verified source and ABIs | [Contracts and Addresses](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/contracts), official manifests, and block explorer links
Audit reports | [Audits and Security](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/audits-security)
Final user parameters | The relevant product page and contract reference
Stewardship bounds | [Stewardship, not Governance](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/stewardship)

## Things Polaris will not ask for

- A seed phrase or private key.
- KYC for protocol use.
- A direct transfer to a support wallet.
- Interaction with an unpublished address.
- Trust in a frontend that is not linked from official Polaris channels.

## Risk docs

Read [Risk Disclosure](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/risk-disclosure) for smart-contract, collateral, liquidation, peg, Stability Pool, pETH, stewardship, and phishing risks. Read [Audits and Security](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/audits-security) for the security model and audit status.

## Developer verification

For apps and indexers, add explicit allowlists for network IDs and contract addresses. Testnet allowlists should point to Sepolia (`11155111`) artifacts from [Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) and the linked manifests. Production allowlists should stay empty until production addresses are published. Your integration should fail closed if the deployment manifest, ABI, or verified source does not match the official docs.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: official app, app URL, phishing, verify contracts, testnet, mainnet.
