## Deposit to the Polaris Stability Pool

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield/deposit-to-stability-pool

# Deposit to the Polaris Stability Pool

This page walks through depositing a pAsset (pUSD in the examples) into the [Stability Pool](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield/stability-pool), claiming rewards, and withdrawing. If you have not yet, read [The Stability Pool](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield/stability-pool) first — it explains what you earn and the risk you take on.

App anchor: **Earn** tab for direct deposits, or **Zap** tab when the app bundles the purchase and deposit.

> **Actionability:**
> Stability Pool deposits are testnet-actionable only on Sepolia using the current Public Testnet 1 manifests. Production deposits are not mainnet-actionable until [Polaris Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) publishes the official app, production pool addresses, final reward/withdrawal parameters, and audit status. Screenshots and the exact UI flow are forthcoming; the steps below describe the intended flow and will get images at launch.

## Before you start

You need:

- A pAsset to deposit — most commonly **pUSD**. [Mint pUSD](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/minting-passets) first if you hold none.
- ETH for gas.
- An understanding that liquidation gains arrive in **pETH**, and the [risk](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield/stability-pool#the-honest-risk) of a severe crash. For pure pUSD exposure, use a wrapper vault — see [Where the Yield Comes From](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield/yield-sources).

## Before you sign

Before depositing, check:

- Official app URL, network, pAsset, and Stability Pool contract.
- Deposit amount, projected pool share, pool size, and reward tokens.
- Current APR estimate, liquidation parameters, and withdrawal conditions.
- First-loss exposure: liquidations can burn deposited pAssets and replace them with pETH.
- Whether you are comfortable receiving pETH during volatile liquidations.
- Gas, slippage, MEV, and wrapper-vault availability if you plan to convert pETH rewards back to pAsset.
- Approval spender, allowance amount, simulation result, and quote freshness.

> **Warning:**
> A Stability Pool deposit can lose value. Liquidations convert part of your deposited pAsset into pETH, and in a severe or flash crash the pETH you receive can be worth **less** than the pAsset burned — your principal is not protected. Size your deposit accordingly and read the [Risk Disclosure](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/risk-disclosure).

## Deposit

### Open the pool

Open the Polaris app and go to the **Stability Pool** for your pAsset (e.g. the pUSD pool).

### Enter a deposit amount

Enter the amount of pUSD to deposit.

### Approve the spend

**Approve** the pUSD spend, if prompted (a one-time token approval).

### Confirm the deposit

Confirm the **deposit** transaction in your wallet.

### Position is active

Your share of the pool is now active. It accrues borrower-interest yield immediately and is eligible for liquidation gains from the next liquidation onward.

## Claim rewards

Your two reward streams behave differently:

- **Interest share (pUSD)** accrues continuously to your position.
- **Liquidation gains (pETH)** accumulate to your position with each liquidation processed.

To realize them:

### Open your position

In the Stability Pool view, open your position to see **claimable pUSD** and **claimable pETH**.

### Claim or compound

Select **Claim** to withdraw rewards to your wallet, or compound them back into the pool where supported.

### Confirm reward action

Confirm the transaction in your wallet.

## Withdraw

### Choose Withdraw

In the Stability Pool view, choose **Withdraw**.

### Enter a withdrawal amount

Enter the amount of pUSD to remove, or select the full balance.

### Confirm withdrawal

Confirm the transaction in your wallet.

Withdrawing settles any unclaimed rewards. Your deposited pUSD balance fluctuates as liquidations convert part of it into pETH — what you withdraw reflects the pool's current state, not a fixed principal.

## Next steps

- [The Stability Pool](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield/stability-pool) — the concept, in full.
- [Where the Yield Comes From](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield/yield-sources) — every source feeding your yield.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: Earn, earn, yield, APR, Stability Pool, deposit, claim rewards.

---

## Polaris Stability Pool

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield/stability-pool

# Polaris Stability Pool

A Stability Pool deposit is not a fixed pAsset savings balance. Liquidations can burn part of your deposited pAsset and replace it with pETH rewards. Before depositing, check the current pool size, claimable rewards, pETH price exposure, withdrawal conditions, and whether wrapper vaults are available.

App anchor: **Earn** tab -> Stability Pool. Zap may route you into the same final position after bundling WETH -> pAsset -> deposit.

## What it is

The **Stability Pool** is a reserve of pAssets — pUSD, for example — that depositors supply to the protocol. It backstops [liquidations](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations): it holds pUSD on standby so that, when a [trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/managing-your-trove) falls below its minimum collateralization ratio, the protocol can close out that debt.

Each [pAsset](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/passet-catalog) has its own Stability Pool. A pUSD pool backstops pUSD troves, a pGOLD pool backstops pGOLD troves, and so on across the [StablecoinOS](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/core-concepts). One pool per instance bounds the blast radius: a depositor is only ever exposed to liquidations of the pAsset they deposited, never to another pAsset's pool.

## How it processes liquidations

Most CDP designs require a liquidator to bring fresh capital to close a bad position. Polaris does not — the capital is already pooled.

When a trove is liquidated:

1. The pool **burns** its pUSD against the liquidated debt, up to the amount available (a minimum buffer is always kept, so the pool is never fully emptied). Any debt the pool cannot cover is **redistributed** to the remaining troves instead.
2. The liquidated trove's **pETH collateral is paid out** to depositors, pro rata to their share of the pool.

Because liquidations carry a penalty, the pETH paid out is worth *more* than the pUSD burned. A depositor's pUSD is converted into pETH at a discount to market — typically a net gain.

Liquidation gains arrive as <strong>pETH</strong>, not pUSD. If you want pure pUSD exposure, wrapper vaults that convert that pETH back to pUSD will be available at or shortly after launch. See Where the Yield Comes From.

## Why it is the primary yield venue

Depositing in the Stability Pool earns two uncorrelated streams:

Stream | Paid in | Character

**Share of borrower interest** | pUSD | Regular, continuous, non-spiky
**Liquidation gains** | pETH | Event-driven, spiky, arrives with volatility

The interest share is the steady base; liquidation gains spike precisely when markets are turbulent. Together they make the Stability Pool the protocol's **primary yield venue** for pAssets — and the foundation other yield products build on. The share of borrower interest routed to depositors is [stewarded](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/stewardship) within hardcoded bounds (a floor, to keep the pool funded for liquidations; a ceiling, to keep pUSD liquid across DeFi); exact values are finalized closer to launch.

## Pre-launch parameter status

Parameter | Status | Reader impact

Borrower-interest share to Stability Pool | Pending final stewarded bounds and launch value | Determines steady pAsset-denominated yield
Liquidation penalty / depositor discount | Pending final values | Determines expected pETH upside on healthy liquidations
Reward claim and compounding behavior | Pending final UI and contract details | Determines whether rewards accrue separately or auto-settle
Wrapper vault availability | Expected at or shortly after launch; final availability pending | Determines whether users can automate pETH-to-pAsset conversion
Withdrawal constraints | Pending final implementation | Determines how quickly and under what conditions deposits can exit
pETH pricing and execution assumptions | Pending final audited paths | Determines realized value when rewards are sold or wrapped

## Worked example: Stability Pool P&L

Suppose you deposit 10,000 pUSD into a 1,000,000 pUSD Stability Pool, giving you 1% of the pool. A liquidation burns 100,000 pUSD from the pool and pays out pETH worth \$110,000 at execution.

Item | Pool-level amount | Your 1% share

pUSD burned | 100,000 pUSD | 1,000 pUSD
pETH received at execution value | \$110,000 | \$1,100
Gross liquidation gain before price movement | \$10,000 | \$100

That is the healthy-liquidation case: part of your pUSD position converted into discounted pETH. If pETH falls 15% before you sell or wrap it, your \$1,100 of pETH becomes \$935 — a \$65 loss versus the 1,000 pUSD burned. Interest income and later liquidations can offset losses, but do not remove pETH price risk.

## The honest risk

Depositing is not free of risk.

In a severe or flash crash, a trove can fall below 100% collateralization before liquidation completes. If that happens, the pETH you receive can be worth <strong>less</strong> than the pUSD you contributed. The liquidation penalty makes a net gain the normal case, not a guaranteed one. Size your deposit accordingly, and see the Risk Disclosure.

Your liquidation gains are also denominated in pETH, which is [volatile against ETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price). The wrapper vaults above mitigate that exposure, but at the base layer you accept pETH.

## Next steps

- [Deposit to the Stability Pool](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield/deposit-to-stability-pool) — the step-by-step.
- [Where the Yield Comes From](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield/yield-sources) — every source feeding pAsset yield.

Next steps:
- [Deposit to the Stability Pool](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield/deposit-to-stability-pool): The step-by-step guide to depositing.
- [Where the Yield Comes From](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield/yield-sources): Every source feeding pAsset yield.
- [Liquidations](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations): Understand how the pool absorbs liquidations.

---

[pUSD: No Counterparty, No Ceiling](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/pusd-no-counterparty/)
How pUSD delivers counterparty-free yield that scales with protocol adoption — and the ecosystem taking shape around it.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: Earn, earn, yield, APR, Stability Pool, deposit, claim rewards.

---

## Polaris Yield Sources

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield/yield-sources

# Polaris Yield Sources

Most stablecoin yield is **rented** from offchain sources — T-bills, CEX lending, custodial strategies — which means counterparty risk and a hard ceiling: your yield is capped at whatever that counterparty delivers. Polaris yield is **owned**. Every source is onchain, internal to the protocol, and grows with usage.

> Adoption ⇒ activity ⇒ velocity ⇒ fees ⇒ yield ⇒ more demand.

## The sources

Source | Where it originates | Flows to

**Borrower interest** | Interest [trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/managing-your-trove) owners pay on minted pAssets | Primarily [Stability Pool](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield/stability-pool) depositors; a stewarded split (each floored at 25%) can also fund POLAR stakers and the ecosystem fund, with secondary interest rebates pro-rata to troves
**Liquidation gains** | The penalty on liquidated troves, paid in discounted pETH | Stability Pool depositors
**Bonding curve swap fees** | Fees on every ETH ⇄ pETH swap, buffered by the Fee Router and later floor-sold and burned to raise the [floor price](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price) | pETH holders / troves
**Conversion gains** | ETH released when pETH is burned to mint POLAR in a [conversion auction](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions) | pETH holders / troves
**PolarEX swap fees** | Trading fees from Polaris's integrated DEX, [PolarEX](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/core-concepts) | Troves, POLAR Staking, or future vePOLAR paths where launch configuration exposes them

The first two drive [Stability Pool](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield/stability-pool) yield and are the primary venue for pAsset holders. The bonding curve and conversion components make **pETH itself yield-bearing**, which is why using pETH as collateral can lower a borrower's net effective cost.

## Why this is "self-scaling"

None of these sources depends on an external rate environment. They depend on **activity**:

- More troves opened ⇒ more borrower interest.
- More volatility ⇒ more liquidation gains.
- More trading ⇒ more bonding curve and PolarEX swap fees.
- More POLAR minted ⇒ more conversion gains.

Yield is therefore tied to protocol adoption, not to a counterparty's balance sheet. The mechanism scales with pAsset supply, but realized yield still depends on actual activity, rates, liquidations, contract execution, and market conditions. That is what "self-scaling" means: **activity ⇒ yield**, with no offchain dependency to compress or disappear.

## Owned, not rented

Polaris yield is not subsidized, bridged in, or discretionarily voted into being. It emerges from the protocol's own usage and is captured by the protocol itself — *internalized, not rented*. Because the core is intended to be immutable, no admin key should be able to swap these onchain sources for a T-bill desk under pressure after audited deployment; that architecture is designed to avoid the [degradation pattern](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/why-polaris).

## How yield is distributed

Onchain fees do not flow straight to recipients: conversion proceeds and swap fees go to the **[Fee Router](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/fee-router)**, which buffers them and distributes pETH yield to troves pro-rata to recorded debt. POLAR Staking rewards and future [vePOLAR](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/vepolar) locker rewards are separate from that pETH stream. The stewarded primary-interest split that funds the Stability Pool, POLAR stakers, and the ecosystem fund is a distinct, pAsset-denominated stream. Exact values are finalized closer to launch.

## Pre-launch parameter status

Parameter | Status | Reader impact

Borrower-interest split | Pending final stewarded bounds and launch value | Determines Stability Pool base yield
Liquidation penalty / discount | Pending final values | Determines event-driven pETH gains for depositors
Bonding-curve swap fee | Pending final audited value | Determines pETH floor growth from swaps
Conversion-auction fee flow | Pending final audited implementation | Determines pETH-holder and trove yield from conversions
PolarEX fee routing | Pending final implementation | Determines split between troves, POLAR Staking, and future vePOLAR paths
Fee Router cadence and buffers | Pending final implementation | Determines how often yield is distributed and how much is retained as buffer

---

[pUSD: No Counterparty, No Ceiling](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/pusd-no-counterparty/)
How pUSD delivers counterparty-free yield that scales with protocol adoption — and the ecosystem taking shape around it.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: Earn, earn, yield, APR, Stability Pool, deposit, claim rewards.

---

## Polaris Redemptions and Liquidations

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations

# Polaris Redemptions and Liquidations

This section explains the mechanisms that keep pAssets backed and near peg. They are not optional background for borrowers or Stability Pool depositors.

## Read in order

Page | What it answers

[pAsset Redemptions](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/redemptions) | How below-peg pAssets redeem for pETH collateral, and how that affects every open trove for that pAsset.
[Polaris Liquidations](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations) | When a trove is forced closed and how the Stability Pool or redistribution absorbs it.
[Polaris Recovery Mode](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode) | What changes when system-wide reserve backing runs thin.

## Launch status

Exact fees, penalties, thresholds, dust rules, keeper incentives, and oracle rules are pending final audited deployment. Use Public Testnet 1 only for testing, and check [Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) before any production action.

## Related pages

- [Managing a Polaris Trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/managing-your-trove) for borrower controls.
- [Polaris Stability Pool](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield/stability-pool) for depositor exposure.
- [Risk Disclosure](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/risk-disclosure) for the full risk catalog.

---

## Polaris Liquidations

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations

# Polaris Liquidations

Borrowers: do not manage a trove from the liquidation line. Check the app's current minimum ICR, Recovery Mode status, accrued interest, collateral-price freshness, and gas conditions before withdrawing collateral or minting more debt. Stability Pool depositors: liquidations convert part of your pAsset deposit into pETH exposure.

## What a liquidation is

A **liquidation** closes a trove that has fallen below its **minimum collateralization ratio** — the point at which its collateral no longer comfortably covers its debt. The protocol cancels the trove's debt and seizes its pETH collateral.

The purpose is solvency. Every pAsset is a claim on collateral; leaving undercollateralized troves open would leave the pAsset not fully backed. Liquidations are how Polaris is designed to keep backing intact, subject to functioning contracts, live Ethereum execution, available gas, valid pricing data, keeper/executor availability, and audited invariants.

Unlike a [redemption](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/redemptions), a liquidation is not an arbitrage you can opt into at par — it is a forced close triggered by your own ratio crossing a hard line.

## Where the debt and collateral go

Liquidation runs in two tiers.

1. **The Stability Pool absorbs it first.** The [Stability Pool](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield/stability-pool) holds a reserve of the pAsset. When a trove is liquidated, the pool burns pAsset equal to the trove's debt and receives its pETH collateral in return. Because liquidated troves are seized while still *overcollateralized* (above 100%, below the minimum), the pool typically receives more value in pETH than the pAsset it burns. That surplus is the depositors' compensation.

2. **Redistribution is the fallback.** If the Stability Pool is empty or too small to cover the debt, the trove's debt *and* collateral spread across the remaining troves on that pAsset, in proportion to their size. Other borrowers take on a slice of debt and a matching slice of pETH. The system stays solvent either way.

Path | Trigger | Absorbs debt | Receives collateral

**Stability Pool** | Pool has sufficient pAsset | Pool burns its pAsset | Depositors, pro-rata
**Redistribution** | Pool empty / insufficient | Other troves take it on | Other troves, pro-rata

**Collateral surplus.** When the Stability Pool absorbs a liquidation, the trove surrenders only what covers its debt, a liquidation penalty, and gas compensation for the liquidator. Any collateral beyond that goes to the **Collateral Surplus Pool**, where the former borrower can claim it. (Collateral spread through the redistribution fallback is not returned this way.)

## Pre-launch parameter status

Parameter | Status | Reader impact

Minimum ICR per pAsset | Pending final values | Defines when a trove becomes liquidatable in Normal Mode
Liquidation penalty / discount | Pending final values | Determines borrower loss and Stability Pool upside
Gas compensation / keeper incentive | Implemented; final values pending | Affects whether liquidations execute quickly during stress
Stability Pool offset priority | Intended first path; final implementation pending audit | Determines how much debt the pool absorbs before redistribution
Redistribution formula | Pending final implementation | Determines how uncovered debt and collateral spread across remaining troves
Oracle freshness and fallback rules | Pending final audited implementation | Determines whether liquidations pause, continue, or use fallback pricing on stale data
Minimum debt / dust handling | Pending final values | Affects edge cases when troves are nearly closed

## Worked example: liquidation

The numbers below are illustrative only. Suppose the app shows a 120% minimum ICR for a pUSD trove. A borrower has 10,000 pUSD of debt and pETH collateral valued at \$11,800, so the ICR is 118% — below the illustrative minimum, and liquidatable.

If the Stability Pool is large enough, it burns 10,000 pUSD and receives the trove's pETH. The borrower's debt is cleared; the collateral covers the debt, the liquidation penalty, and gas compensation, with any surplus returned for the borrower to claim. Depositors receive pETH pro rata; in a normal overcollateralized liquidation, that pETH should be worth more than the pUSD burned, but the exact gain depends on the final penalty, execution timing, pETH price, gas, and slippage.

If the Stability Pool is empty or too small, the uncovered portion follows the redistribution path: remaining troves inherit a proportional slice of both the liquidated debt and collateral.

## Risks

Liquidation is involuntary and can happen fast. A liquidated trove gives up its collateral to cover the debt plus a liquidation penalty and gas compensation; any surplus is returned to you to claim, but the penalty makes liquidation a real loss to avoid. Keep a <strong>buffer</strong> above the minimum ratio so a sharp move in collateral price does not cross the line. The thinner your margin, the less room you have when volatility hits. See Managing Your Trove.

You are the system's first-loss backstop. In a flash crash, troves can be liquidated near — or in extreme cases below — 100% collateralization, and you may receive pETH worth less than the pAsset burned. Over a full cycle the surplus from healthy liquidations compensates for that risk, but it is not riskless yield. See The Stability Pool and the Risk Disclosure.

Exact liquidation thresholds, penalties, and the minimum collateralization ratio per pAsset are finalized closer to launch.

## How liquidation interacts with system health

A trove's individual ratio is not all that matters. When system-wide backing runs thin, the protocol enters [Recovery Mode](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode), which tightens risk-increasing borrower operations while it lasts. Liquidation eligibility is unchanged: a trove is liquidated whenever its ICR falls below the minimum, in every mode. Recovery Mode is the system-level safeguard complementing the trove-level liquidation described here.

---

Liquidations are not punishment — they are the mechanism designed to keep a pAsset backed by the collateral it claims. Keep a buffer, and you are less likely to lose your trove.

Next steps:
- [Managing Your Trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/managing-your-trove): Keep your ICR healthy and avoid liquidation.
- [Stability Pool](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield/stability-pool): Understand how the pool absorbs liquidations.
- [Recovery Mode](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode): The system-level safeguard for thin backing.

---

[CDPs Mint Dollars. Polaris Mints Anything](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/polaris-mints-anything/)
From Maker to Liquity, CDP models shaped DeFi lending. Polaris takes the blueprint further with pETH, self-adjusting rates, and a factory for stablecoins pegged to anything.

---

## Polaris Recovery Mode

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode

# Polaris Recovery Mode

## The Total Collateralization Ratio

The **Total Collateralization Ratio (TCR)** is the system-wide health metric for a pAsset. It is not a simple market-value-of-collateral over debt figure: it measures the bonding-curve reserve backing all the pETH collateral — the collateral scaled by the curve's reserve-backing fraction and valued at the reserve (redemption) price — divided by total pAsset debt outstanding.

$$
\text{TCR} = \frac{\text{Reserve backing the collateral} \times \text{reserve price}}{\text{Total debt}}
$$

A high TCR means a deep, well-backed system; a low TCR means the reserve behind that pAsset is thin relative to its debt. In the contracts this is the reserve-to-debt ratio, and crossing the threshold triggers the protective state (internally called Emergency Mode).

## What Recovery Mode is

**Recovery Mode** is a protective state the protocol enters automatically when the TCR drops below a set threshold. It is not a freeze and not an emergency shutdown; it tightens the rules to make the system **harder to destabilize** while collateral is scarce.

The purpose is system-level solvency. When the TCR is low, a single sharp move could tip the whole pAsset undercollateralized. Recovery Mode raises the bar for risky behavior so the system pulls back toward health instead of sliding further.

## Pre-launch parameter status

Parameter | Status | Reader impact

Recovery Mode TCR trigger | Pending final value | Defines when system-wide rules tighten
Recovery Mode exit condition | Pending final implementation | Defines when Normal Mode resumes
Recovery Mode borrower-operation minimum | Pending final per-pAsset value | The minimum ICR collateral-reducing or debt-increasing actions must meet while the protective state is active
Restricted borrower actions | Pending final implementation | Determines which mints, withdrawals, or adjustments are blocked
Fee / incentive changes in Recovery Mode | Pending final launch decision | Affects redeemers, liquidators, and borrower behavior during stress

## How behavior changes

Recovery Mode does **not** change which troves are liquidatable. In every mode, a trove is liquidatable only once its ICR falls below the minimum (the MCR). What tightens is the set of **borrower actions** allowed while the protective state is active.

Your action | Normal Mode | Recovery Mode

**Add collateral / repay debt** | Allowed | Allowed
**Withdraw collateral / mint more debt** | Allowed if ICR stays above the minimum | Allowed only if the resulting ICR clears a higher recovery minimum and does not decrease your ICR
**Liquidation eligibility** | ICR below the minimum | ICR below the minimum (unchanged)

The principle: Recovery Mode restricts actions that would make the system thinner, while always allowing actions that strengthen it. Liquidation eligibility stays exactly where it is in Normal Mode; only risk-increasing operations change, now clearing a higher bar and never lowering your ICR. The net effect pushes every participant toward adding collateral and trimming debt — exactly what raises the system's backing above the threshold and exits Recovery Mode.

> **Note:** The exact threshold that triggers Recovery Mode, and the precise recovery-mode action minimums, are finalized closer to launch. The table above shows the model, not final parameters.

## Worked example: Recovery Mode

The numbers below are illustrative only. The trigger is the system's reserve-backing-to-debt ratio, so a plain collateral-value over debt division is only a rough proxy; treat this as shape, not arithmetic.

Suppose the protective state is active for pUSD. A trove at 130% ICR is **not** liquidated by Recovery Mode: liquidation eligibility is unchanged, so any trove above the minimum stays safe in every mode. What changes is the borrower's freedom to act. While Recovery Mode is active you cannot withdraw collateral or mint more debt unless the result clears the higher recovery minimum and does not lower your ICR; adding collateral and repaying debt are always allowed.

The borrower's defense is the same as always: add collateral, repay debt, or both. For example, a trove with \$13,000 of collateral and 10,000 pUSD of debt is at 130%; repaying 2,000 pUSD moves it to \$13,000 / 8,000 pUSD, or 162.5%, before fees and price movement — and a healthier ICR also widens your room to act while the system is stressed.

## How to make your trove safe

If the system is in or near Recovery Mode, the move is the same one that always protects a trove: raise your **ICR**.

1. **Add pETH collateral.** More collateral against the same debt lifts your ICR directly.
2. **Repay debt.** Burning pAsset to reduce what you owe lifts your ICR from the other side.
3. **Do both early.** Recovery Mode restricts risk-increasing actions and hurts most when you are forced to react under stress, so build the buffer *before* stress arrives, not during it.

See [Managing Your Trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/managing-your-trove) for the mechanics, and [Liquidations](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations) for what happens to a trove that falls below the line.

> **Warning:** Recovery Mode is most likely to bite during exactly the volatile conditions when adding collateral is hardest. The only reliable defense is a wide buffer maintained in calm markets. See the [Risk Disclosure](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/risk-disclosure).

---

Recovery Mode is the system protecting itself: when backing runs thin, the rules on risk-increasing actions tighten until health returns. Keep your ICR high and your buffer wide, and you keep the freedom to act through the stress.

---

## pAsset Redemptions

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/redemptions

# pAsset Redemptions

> **Actionability:** Redemptions are testnet-actionable only on Sepolia using test assets and current manifests. Production redemptions are not mainnet-actionable until [Polaris Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) publishes the official app, production addresses, final fee formula, dust rules, and audit status.

> **Before you act:**
> Redemptions are economic transactions, not guaranteed profit. Check the live fee quote, pETH execution price, gas, expected slippage, possible MEV, and whether the trade stays profitable if conditions change before inclusion. Borrowers should know that a redemption applies collectively and pro-rata across all open troves' collateral, not to a single lowest-ICR trove first, so a below-peg pAsset shrinks every open position carrying that pAsset's debt regardless of its ICR.

## What a redemption is

A **redemption** exchanges a pAsset for the **[pETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/bonding-curve)** collateral backing it at the pAsset's protocol reference value, minus a fee. It pays when the pAsset trades *below* peg by more than the fee, gas, slippage, and execution risk.

If pUSD slips to \$0.98, a redeemer can buy pUSD cheaply, redeem it for about \$1 of pETH minus the fee, and sell or hold the pETH. The pETH comes from the collateral of troves carrying that pUSD debt, and their debt shrinks by the pUSD redeemed. No counterparty agrees to the trade; the protocol enforces it.

This puts an arbitrage floor under the pAsset's market price.

## Pre-launch parameter status

Parameter | Status | Reader impact

Redemption fee formula | Pending final audited implementation | Determines redeemer net proceeds and final collateral movement
Fee decay / update cadence | Pending final implementation | Affects timing and repeated-redemption profitability
Partial-redemption dust rules | Pending final values | Determines whether tiny trove remnants are allowed or closed
Per-transaction caps / cooldowns | Pending final launch decision | Affects large redemptions and MEV surface
Collateral pricing path | Pending final audited implementation | Determines how much pETH corresponds to the redeemed debt value

## How it restores the peg

A below-peg pAsset is a standing arbitrage. Walk the loop:

1. **Buy** pUSD on the market at \$0.98.
2. **Redeem** it for \$1 − fee of pETH.
3. **Sell** the pETH, pocket the spread.
4. Repeat until the arb closes.

Each pass buys pUSD on the open market, applying **upward price pressure**. Arbitrageurs keep going until pUSD is back at peg and the trade stops paying. Self-interest restores the peg, not intervention.

> **Note:** Redemptions handle the *below*-peg case. The *above*-peg case is handled by the inverse mechanism, **minting**, where arbitrageurs add pETH to troves to mint pAssets when the pAsset trades over peg. See [Minting pAssets](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/minting-passets).

The two mechanisms are mirror images:

Mechanism | Direction | Arbitrageur does | Effect on troves

**Redemption** | pAsset < peg | Burns pAsset (repays debt) ⇒ takes pETH | Debt and collateral both shrink
**Minting** | pAsset > peg | Adds pETH ⇒ mints pAsset | Debt and collateral both grow

Their volumes also serve as the protocol's peg signal: a spike in redemptions reads as a downward depeg, a spike in mintings as an upward one. That is how Polaris sets [autonomous interest rates](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/minting-passets) without a direct pUSD market-price feed.

## Mechanics in detail

### Fee formula

The final redemption-fee formula is pending. The user-facing rule is simple: the redeemer receives the redeemed amount's reference value in pETH **minus** the fee and execution costs. If pUSD trades at \$0.98 but the fee, gas, pETH slippage, and MEV risk exceed the \$0.02 spread, the redemption is unprofitable.

The app should show a pre-confirmation quote once the interface is live. Treat examples here as arithmetic only.

### How redemptions are distributed

Redemptions target no specific trove, and there is no sorted ICR queue. A redemption applies **collectively** across every open trove on that pAsset at once. The protocol adjusts a global mint/redeem accounting product, and each trove's share of the redeemed debt and of the pETH removed is proportional to its recorded collateral. A larger trove absorbs a larger absolute share; ICR moves no trove ahead of or behind any other. Adding pETH collateral or repaying debt changes your trove's size, but moves you out of no queue, because none exists.

### Partials

A redemption is sized by the pAsset the redeemer burns, not matched against any single trove. The protocol distributes the debt reduction and the corresponding pETH outflow proportionally across all open troves at once, by each trove's collateral share, in a single system-wide update. The only fill limits: you cannot redeem the entire outstanding debt (the call reverts if the requested pAsset is greater than or equal to total system debt), and the system must stay above its minimum total collateral. Final minimum-debt and dust rules are pending.

### Borrower effects

A redemption repays your debt with your own collateral, so what changes is not your net equity (roughly unchanged at reference prices before fees, rounding, and final fee-routing) but your *control*: your trove shrinks, your pETH exposure shrinks, and your future pETH-linked yield potential falls.

Because redemptions remove equal reference value from both sides of an overcollateralized trove, the remaining ICR often rises after a partial redemption. That does not make redemptions harmless; it makes them an involuntary deleveraging event, not a liquidation penalty.

### MEV and transaction ordering

Redemptions are visible onchain transactions. Searchers can compete for the same spread, the pETH exit price can move, and the system's totals can shift before your transaction lands. Use the app's slippage limits, fee quotes, and any private-transaction support.

## Who gets redeemed against

A redemption applies collectively across **all** open troves carrying that pAsset's debt. Each trove's debt and pETH collateral are reduced in proportion to its recorded collateral, through the protocol's global mint/redeem accounting. There is no per-trove [Individual Collateralization Ratio (ICR)](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/glossary) ranking, no "riskiest trove first," and no queue: redemption does not single out the lowest-ICR troves.

Being redeemed against is not a liquidation loss: your debt is repaid at face value with your own collateral, so your net equity is broadly unchanged before fees and rounding. But it is not free either: it closes part of your position involuntarily, at a moment you did not choose.

A higher collateralization ratio protects you against **liquidation**, but does **not** exempt you from redemptions. Every open trove on that pAsset absorbs a proportional share of each redemption regardless of ICR. Because the split is by collateral, a larger trove absorbs a larger absolute amount, but no level of ICR moves you out of range.

> **Note:** Polaris adds borrower-protection details to the naive redemption design. Exact mechanics, including fee routing, dust handling, and any caps or cooldowns, are finalized closer to launch.

## Worked example: redemption impact

Assume pUSD trades at \$0.98, the redemption fee quote is 0.5%, and gas/slippage are ignored. A redeemer buys 10,000 pUSD for \$9,800 and redeems it for \$10,000 of pETH minus the 0.5% fee, or about \$9,950 of pETH. The gross spread is about \$150 before gas, slippage, MEV, and price movement.

Now the borrower side. A 4,000 pUSD redemption applies **collectively**: it reduces total system debt by 4,000 pUSD and removes roughly \$4,000 of pETH from the pool, both reductions shared across every open trove in proportion to its recorded collateral. No single trove has its debt cut from 12,000 to 8,000; each trove absorbs only its proportional slice.

For intuition, here is the proportional share for one trove holding a fifth of the system's collateral, so it absorbs a fifth of the redemption (800 pUSD of debt and about \$800 of pETH):

Step | Collateral value | Debt | ICR

Before redemption | \$18,000 | 12,000 pUSD | 150%
After absorbing its pro-rata share | About \$17,200 | 11,200 pUSD | About 154%

The borrower did not pay off debt voluntarily; the market did it through the redemption path. The trove is slightly safer by ICR, but smaller and less exposed to pETH, as is every other open trove on that pAsset.

## For borrowers

> **Before you act:**
> A redemption can reduce your trove **while it is still solvent**; there is no minimum ICR to cross. Whenever the pAsset trades below peg, every open trove backing it is redeemed against proportionally to its collateral share, shrinking your position and pETH exposure without your consent. ICR governs liquidation safety, not redemption order, so a higher collateralization ratio does not exempt you. See the [Risk Disclosure](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/risk-disclosure).

1. Track your **ICR** in absolute terms and keep a comfortable buffer above the MCR; a redemption can touch your position whenever the pAsset is below peg.
2. Redemptions apply pro-rata across all troves backing that pAsset, so there is no ICR queue to climb out of; raising your ICR protects against liquidation, not redemption.
3. Treat a below-peg pAsset as a live signal: that is exactly when redemptions get worked.
4. Interest accrual raises your debt over time, eroding your ICR even if collateral prices are flat.

See [Managing Your Trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/managing-your-trove) for the levers, and the [Risk Disclosure](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/risk-disclosure) for the full picture.

---

A redemption is the market repricing your pAsset back to par through borrower collateral and arbitrage incentives. It reaches every open trove proportionally, so keep your buffer wide and treat below-peg conditions as the moment your position can shrink.

[CDPs Mint Dollars. Polaris Mints Anything](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/polaris-mints-anything/)
From Maker to Liquity, CDP models shaped DeFi lending. Polaris takes the blueprint further with pETH, self-adjusting rates, and a factory for stablecoins pegged to anything.

---

## Polaris POLAR, Staking, and Conversion

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar

# Polaris POLAR, Staking, and Conversion

POLAR is the stewardship token. After the initial distribution, new POLAR is intended to enter only when pETH is burned through conversion, not through emissions.

> **Status distinction:** The app may expose **POLAR Staking** as a current stake/unstake/claim surface on testnet. **vePOLAR locking** is separate and forthcoming: lock durations, vote-power decay, voting rules, and production addresses are pending audited deployment.

## Start here if

You want to... | Start with

Understand what POLAR is for | [POLAR Token](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/polar-token)
Compare current staking with future vePOLAR | [POLAR Tokenomics](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/tokenomics)
Understand pETH -> POLAR conversion | [POLAR Conversion Auctions](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions)
Prepare to sign a conversion | [Participate in a POLAR Conversion](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/participate-in-conversion)

## Read in order

Page | What it answers

[POLAR Token](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/polar-token) | What POLAR is for and why conversion links it to pETH.
[POLAR Tokenomics](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/tokenomics) | Issuance structure, current staking, future vePOLAR, and what is still pending.

## Launch status

POLAR, conversion, staking, and vePOLAR addresses are pending production publication. Do not use a conversion, staking, or locking interface unless [Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) and [Contracts & Addresses](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/contracts) point to it.
